sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Hypothermia occurs when the body loses heat faster than it can produce it causing the body temperature to drop below 35°C.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Mild hypothermia (32-35°C): shivering, lethargy, weakness, slurred speech, ataxia
- Moderate hypothermia (30-32°C): decreasing level of consciousness, reduced respiratory rate, shivering may lessen or stop, muscle rigidity, poor reflexes
- Severe hypothermia (<30°C): hypotension, bradycardia, arrhythmias, flaccidity, fixed or dilated pupils, cardiac arrest

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Handle the patient gently and avoid sudden movements, especially in moderate or severe hypothermia
- Hypothermia develops more rapidly in immersion and in patients with impaired thermoregulation
- Osborne (J) waves and atrial arrhythmias may occur and often improve with rewarming
- Potential major trauma patients should receive active thermal protection irrespective of measured temperature
- Do not massage or rub the patient, as this may worsen heat loss and discomfort
- Avoid unnecessary prolonged scene procedures if they interrupt rewarming, packaging, or transport priorities

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- When safe to do so, remove the patient from the cold environment and protect them from further heat loss
- Position the patient recumbent if appropriate or in a position of comfort
- Vital Sign Survey, including close temperature and BGL monitoring where available
- Consider Oxygen if indicated
- Remove wet clothing and dry the patient gently
- Use passive rewarming with insulation, blankets, a warm environment, and protection from wind or further cold exposure
- Offer warm sweet oral fluids only if the patient is awake, able to swallow safely, and not nauseated
- Secondary Survey

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Gain IV access if indicated
- Obtain a 12 lead ECG
- Consider warmed IV fluids if available and clinically indicated
- Continue active insulation and gentle handling during packaging and transport

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Severe hypothermia with absent or very slow signs of life can be difficult to distinguish from cardiac arrest; assess carefully and follow the hypothermic cardiac arrest guideline when relevant
- In suspected hypothermic cardiac arrest, prioritise high-quality CPR, careful handling, and transport according to local resuscitation guidance
- Defibrillation and vasoactive medications may be less effective at very low temperatures, but resuscitation should continue according to the relevant hypothermic arrest guideline
